quiz_questions.question_text: What is work in physics?

options.text: Force applied over a distance in the direction of motion
The amount of energy an object has
How fast an object is moving
The total force acting on an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Work is defined as W = F x d x cos(theta), where force and displacement are in the same direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a 10 N force moves an object 5 meters, what is the work done?
quiz_questions.explanation: Work = Force x Distance = 10 N x 5 m = 50 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: When is work zero?

options.text: When force is perpendicular to displacement

quiz_questions.explanation: Work is zero when the force and displacement are perpendicular (cos 90 degrees = 0).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is energy?

options.text: The time it takes to move

quiz_questions.explanation: Energy is the ability to perform work or cause change.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is energy conserved in an isolated system?
quiz_questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to work when force and displacement are parallel?
quiz_questions.explanation: When force and displacement are parallel, cos(0 degrees) = 1, so work = F x d (maximum).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which unit represents energy?
quiz_questions.explanation: Energy is measured in Joules (J), the same unit as work.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does a book sitting on a table do work?
quiz_questions.explanation: Work requires both force and displacement in the direction of the force. A stationary book has no displacement.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is work related to energy?

options.text: Work and energy are the same thing
Work has no relationship to energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Work is the process by which energy is transferred between objects or transformed.

quiz_questions.question_text: If negative work is done on an object, what happens?
quiz_questions.explanation: Negative work means the force opposes motion, so the object's energy decreases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'power'?

options.text: The rate at which work is done or energy is transferred
The total amount of work performed
How quickly an object is moving
The force applied to an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Power is the amount of work or energy transfer per unit time (P = W/t).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'kinetic energy'?

options.text: Energy an object possesses due to its motion
Energy stored in an object's position
The maximum force an object can exert

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy depends on the motion of an object: KE = (1/2)mv^2.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'potential energy'?

options.text: Energy stored due to an object's position or configuration
Energy an object loses when moving

quiz_questions.explanation: Potential energy is energy stored by virtue of an object's position, such as gravitational potential energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'mechanical energy'?

options.text: The sum of kinetic and potential energy in a system
Energy from a machine or motor
The work done by all forces

quiz_questions.explanation: Mechanical energy = KE + PE (kinetic + potential energy).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'dissipative forces'?

options.text: Forces that remove mechanical energy from a system (like friction)
Forces that add energy to a system

quiz_questions.explanation: Dissipative forces, such as friction and air resistance, convert mechanical energy into heat.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'displacement'?

options.text: The straight-line distance and direction from start to end position

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is a vector quantity representing the change in position.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'conservative force'?

options.text: A force for which work done is independent of the path taken
A force that always opposes motion
A force that is always constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Examples of conservative forces include gravity and springs; work depends only on initial and final positions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'non-conservative force'?

options.text: A force for which work done depends on the path taken
A force that is always parallel to motion
A force that is always opposing motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction and air resistance are non-conservative forces; work depends on the actual path taken.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'work-energy theorem'?

options.text: The net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy
Work is always equal to potential energy
Energy is always conserved without exception
Power multiplied by time equals work

quiz_questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem states: W_net = delta_KE.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student pushes a 50 kg box across a floor with a force of 100 N for 10 meters. How much work was done?
quiz_questions.explanation: W = F x d = 100 N x 10 m = 1000 J (force is in direction of motion).

quiz_questions.question_text: A 2 kg object is lifted 3 meters against gravity. How much gravitational potential energy is gained? (g = 10 m/s^2)
quiz_questions.explanation: PE = mgh = 2 kg x 10 m/s^2 x 3 m = 60 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car traveling at 20 m/s has a mass of 1500 kg. What is its kinetic energy?
If 200 J of work is done on a 5 kg object initially at rest, what is its final velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: Using work-energy theorem: W = (1/2)mv^2 -> 200 = (1/2) x 5 x v^2 -> v^2 = 80 -> v approximately 8.94 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A motor does 5000 J of work in 10 seconds. What is its power?
quiz_questions.explanation: Power = Work / Time = 5000 J / 10 s = 500 W.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object falls from rest and lands with a kinetic energy of 500 J. If its mass is 10 kg, from what height did it fall? (g = 10 m/s^2)
quiz_questions.explanation: PE = KE at impact -> mgh = 500 -> 10 x 10 x h = 500 -> h = 5 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 20 N force is applied at 60 degrees to the direction of motion over 5 meters. How much work is done?
quiz_questions.explanation: W = F x d x cos(60 degrees) = 20 x 5 x 0.5 = 50 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spring is compressed, storing 1000 J of elastic potential energy. If released, what is the maximum speed of a 4 kg object attached to it?
quiz_questions.explanation: PE = KE -> 1000 = (1/2) x 4 x v^2 -> v^2 = 500 -> v approximately 22.4 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object experiences both an applied force of 50 N and friction of 10 N over 8 meters. What is the net work done?
quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = 50 - 10 = 40 N. Net work = 40 N x 8 m = 320 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1500 kg car accelerates from 0 to 25 m/s. How much work was done by the engine?
